channel.title: Terms on emsenn.net
channel.description: Recent content in Terms on emsenn.net

item.title: Change
item.description:
Change is the transition of a thing from one state to another. A leaf turns from green to brown. A task moves from pending to complete. A river shifts its course. For something to be changing, something must change—and for change to be real, something else must remain the same.

¶Four Types of Change

Aristotle’s Physics (Book III) distinguishes four types of change (metabole), and the distinction still holds. Not all changes are alike.

item.title: Event
item.description:
An event is something that happens at a specific time — a change that can be dated and identified. A window breaking, a patient’s fever spiking, a vote being cast, a bell ringing. These are events. Events mark the transition of a thing from one state to another.

Jaegwon Kim gave the clearest structural account: an event is the exemplification of a property by an object at a time. Write it as [object, property, time]. The window (object) broke (property: intact → shattered) at 3pm (time). The patient (object) developed hypertension (property change) on Monday (time). This is clean and compositional — you can build events from independent parts.

item.title: Identity
item.description:
Identity is what makes a thing THIS thing and not another — what persists through change. Your body replaces nearly every cell over seven years, yet you are still you. The Ship of Theseus: replace every plank, is it still the same ship? Identity is not trivial. It is not given in advance. Identity must be explained, grounded, constituted.

¶The Identity of Indiscernibles

Gottfried Leibniz’s principle of the identity of indiscernibles states the classical answer: if two things are absolutely identical in ALL their properties and ALL their relations, then they ARE the same thing. Conversely: if two things differ in ANY property or relation, they are distinct.

item.title: Property
item.description:
A property is what a thing HAS whether or not anyone observes it — an intrinsic feature rather than an observed quality. A knife is sharp whether or not anyone cuts with it. A diamond is hard whether or not anyone tests its hardness. The property persists independent of observation. Quality can be observer-dependent (a color looks different under different light); a property is intrinsic to the thing itself.

¶Categorical Properties: What Something IS

David Armstrong’s Universals and Scientific Realism (1978) makes a fundamental distinction that clarifies properties. A categorical property is what a thing currently IS — its intrinsic state, observable right now. A knife’s shape is categorical. The blade IS sharp-edged, pointed, curved. A crystal’s structure is categorical: it IS cubic or hexagonal. A person’s height is categorical: they ARE six feet tall. These properties are definite, current, determinate. When you observe a categorical property, you see what the thing is, independent of what it does.

item.title: Relation
item.description: Note on grounding (2026-04-05): The 561 Group’s relationality is rooted in Lakota epistemology, not in the Western tradition surveyed below. See Lakota philosophy and the relationality domain. In Lakota thought (mitákuye oyás’iŋ — “all my relatives”), relating is prior to things: what exists are acts of relating sustained in kinship with land, ancestors, other-than-human beings, and ceremony. The Western tradition below — Aristotle, Russell, Bradley, Whitehead, James, Peirce — is recorded as contrast material, useful for triangulating how European metaphysics arrived at neighbouring but distinct positions. It is not the foundation. The foundation is relational kinship epistemology; the Western survey is how that foundation looks from outside.

item.title: Time
item.description: Time is the dimension in which change occurs and events are ordered — what makes “before” and “after” meaningful. Without time, there is no change, no events, no history. A thing could have no past and no future. It could only be what it is right now, forever. Augustine captured this in Confessions XI: “What then is time? If no one asks me, I know; if I want to explain it, I do not know.”

item.title: Authenticity
item.description: Authenticity is the claim that a person, practice, or community expresses its true nature — that it is what it really is, uncontaminated by external influence, faithful to an original form. The concept has roots in Existentialist philosophy (Heidegger, Sartre) but its political deployment belongs to Romantic nationalism: the claim that each people has an authentic spirit (Volksgeist) expressed in authentic practices, and that these practices must be preserved against the corruptions of modernity.

item.title: Endoxa
item.description:
Endoxa are reputable opinions: propositions accepted by all, by most, or by recognized authorities. In Aristotelian dialectic, they provide starting points for inquiry, not final guarantees of truth [@aristotle-logic-sep].

The methodological role of endoxa is twofold:

  1. They provide shared premises that make argument possible.
  2. They provide targets for criticism, refinement, or rejection when contradictions or counter-cases appear.

Using endoxa means beginning from historically available judgment while keeping those judgments revisable through argument.

item.title: Modernity
item.description:
Modernity names the historical condition characterized by industrialization, urbanization, secularization, bureaucratic rationalization, the nation-state, mass media, and the global circulation of capital. More precisely, it names the claim that these processes constitute progress — a directional movement from tradition toward something better, or at least something inevitable.

The claim generates a binary: modern or traditional, progressive or backward, civilized or primitive. This binary is the engine of both colonial domination (Indigenous peoples classified as “pre-modern” and therefore available for displacement) and Romantic nationalism (the reaction that valorizes tradition, folk life, and organic community against industrial alienation). Both poles depend on modernity’s self-understanding as a break with the past. The colonizer says: we have broken through, they have not. The Romantic nationalist says: the break was a catastrophe, and we must recover what was lost.

item.title: Volksgeist
item.description: Volksgeist is Johann Gottfried Herder’s term for the unique spirit of a people, expressed through its particular language, songs, customs, myths, and material practices. Against the Enlightenment universalism of his contemporaries, Herder argued (1784) that there is no single human nature but only particular peoples, each with its own way of being in the world. The Volk is not a political unit (that is the state) but a cultural one: the organic community that speaks, sings, and tells stories in a way no other community does.

item.title: Epistemic Universalism
item.description: Epistemic universalism is the assumption that for knowledge to be legitimate, it must be articulable in terms that any rational agent could, in principle, accept. It grounds validity in universality: a claim is warranted only if the criteria supporting it are available to everyone, regardless of position, culture, or relational context. This appears as a protection against arbitrary authority — no one is bound by claims they cannot evaluate on shared terms. In practice, it functions as a gatekeeping mechanism that delegitimizes every form of knowing that depends on particular relations, places, or practices.

item.title: Teleology
item.description:
Teleology is explanation or organization structured by an end. A teleological system is one where purpose precedes action — where what something is for determines what it does, what counts as success, and what counts as deviation. The acorn grows toward the oak. The policy is evaluated by whether it achieves its stated goal. The community is organized toward the society it intends to become.

The concept originates in Aristotle’s four causes, where the telos (final cause) of a thing is its purpose or end-state. For Aristotle, understanding a thing means understanding what it is for: the knife exists to cut, the eye exists to see, the polis exists to enable the good life. This framework dominated Western natural philosophy until the early modern period, when mechanistic explanation displaced final causes in physics and biology. Darwin’s theory of natural selection provided a non-teleological account of biological adaptation: organisms are not designed for their environments; they are shaped by them, through differential survival, without intention or purpose.

item.title: Area
item.description:
An area is a place where things can be. A room, a field, a street corner, a clearing in a forest, a ship’s deck, a webpage — all areas. TAB defines an area as “a container with exits.” That’s the whole idea: an area holds things, and it connects to other areas through exits.

An area has contents — the things currently in it. A kitchen contains a table, chairs, dishes, a person cooking. A park contains trees, benches, people, squirrels. The contents change as things arrive and leave. The area persists.

item.title: Container
item.description:
A container is anything that holds something else inside it. A box is a container. A bag is a container. A room, a drawer, a pocket, a folder, a cell, a shipping container, a zip file — all containers.

The defining feature is an inside and an outside with a boundary between them. The boundary can be rigid (a box), flexible (a bag), permeable (a net), virtual (a directory on a filesystem), or conceptual (a category). What matters is that the boundary creates a distinction between what is in and what is not in.

item.title: Person
item.description:
A person is a thing that can act, move, speak, and be held responsible. People are the things that do things on purpose. A rock sits where it is. A person walks somewhere because they want to go there.

A person is an object — they have mass, take up space, exist physically. A person is mobile — they move between areas under their own power. But a person is more than a mobile object. A person has agency: they choose, they communicate, they make promises, they break them. A cart is mobile but not a person. A robot that moves is mobile but arguable. A sleeping human is still a person — agency is a capacity, not a continuous activity.

item.title: Vessel
item.description:
A vessel is a hollow object made to hold something. A cup is a vessel. A jar is a vessel. A bottle, a bowl, a barrel, a tank, a ship’s hull — all vessels.

A vessel has an inside separated from an outside by a wall. The wall gives the inside its shape. A cup is open at the top. A bottle narrows to a neck. A barrel is closed all around except for a bung. The shape of the inside determines what the vessel can hold and how you get things in and out.

item.title: Room
item.description:
¶Formal definition

A Room is a five-tuple R=(E,A,F,Θ,P)\mathcal{R} = (E, A, F, \Theta, P):

R=(E:Enclosure,  A⊆∂E:AccessPoints,  F:Function,  Θ:Agents→{0,1},  P:Agents×F→ΔDeonticProfile)\mathcal{R} = (E : \mathrm{Enclosure},\; A \subseteq \partial E : \mathrm{AccessPoints},\; F : \mathrm{Function},\; \Theta : \mathrm{Agents} \to \{0,1\},\; P : \mathrm{Agents} \times F \to \Delta\mathrm{DeonticProfile})

where:

  • EE is the enclosure — a bounded, connected, topologically closed interior with int(E)≠∅\mathrm{int}(E) \neq \varnothing; bounded by physical structure (walls, bulkheads, deck/overhead) or normative boundary (a designated operational space); the enclosure is habitable in the functional sense: it supports FF’s performance requirements
  • A⊆∂EA \subseteq \partial E is the set of access points — the designated locations on the enclosure boundary through which agents may enter or exit; AA is non-empty (a fully sealed enclosure is not a room but a vessel, vault, or containment structure); the threshold ∂AE\partial_A E is the normatively significant passage
  • FF is the designated function — the activity, purpose, or role the room is organized and equipped to support; FF is institutional (not merely spatial): the wardroom is organized for officer social/mess functions; the chart room for navigation; the control room for reactor operation; the operating room for surgery; FF distinguishes the room from an undifferentiated enclosure
  • Θ:Agents→{0,1}\Theta : \mathrm{Agents} \to \{0,1\} is the access predicate — the normative condition governing authorized entry; Θ(x)=1\Theta(x) = 1 iff agent xx is authorized to enter; unauthorized entry (Θ(x)=0\Theta(x) = 0) does not activate PP and may itself be a violation
  • P:Agents×F→ΔDeonticProfileP : \mathrm{Agents} \times F \to \Delta\mathrm{DeonticProfile} is the presence effect — the change in deontic profile that authorized presence creates; entering a room can activate role-specific permissions, obligations, and liabilities; P(x,F)P(x, F) specifies what changes for xx by virtue of being in R\mathcal{R} in relation to FF

Five invariants. R\mathcal{R} is a room iff it satisfies:

item.title: Act
item.description:
An act is what a thing does that produces change. Pulling a trigger, healing a patient, writing a sentence, deciding to stop — all acts. An act is the manifestation of a thing’s capacity, the realization of its potential to affect the world or itself.

In Aristotle’s Metaphysics (Book IX), an act is energeia — actuality, being-at-work. Aristotle distinguishes this from dunamis, potentiality — the capacity to act. A doctor has the capacity to heal (dunamis); when healing a patient, the doctor is actually healing (energeia). Potentiality is what something can do; actuality is what it is doing. The distinction cuts deeper than tense. Aristotle distinguishes two types of change: kinesis is incomplete motion toward an end — a runner engaged in running has not yet reached the finish. Energeia is activity complete at every moment of its occurrence — the pleasure of seeing, the joy of thinking are fully realized at every instant, not waiting for completion. This separates acts that are means to an end (kinesis) from acts that are their own end (energeia).

item.title: Condition
item.description: A condition is a specific quality of a thing at a given moment. It is quality indexed by time. A patient’s temperature at 3pm is a condition. A bridge’s structural integrity after the earthquake is a condition. A task’s status right now is a condition. A condition is not the quality itself (which a thing can have timelessly) and not state (which is the composite of all conditions at once). A condition is one quality, at one moment.

item.title: Conversation
item.description:
A conversation is a structured exchange between two or more parties where each contribution responds to what came before. What distinguishes conversation from mere sequence is that each turn is shaped by prior turns — a reply presupposes a prompt, a counter presupposes a claim.

Conversations require a shared medium (spoken language, written text, gesture, signal) and a minimal agreement about turn-taking: that parties alternate, that contributions are relevant to what preceded them, and that the exchange can be opened and closed. Grice’s cooperative principle formalized this — participants are expected to be informative, truthful, relevant, and clear, and violations of these expectations carry meaning (irony, implicature, evasion).

item.title: Definition
item.description:
A definition is a statement that fixes the meaning of a term by specifying what it is. Aristotle’s formula — genus plus differentia — remains the backbone: name the kind, then say what distinguishes this instance from other members of that kind. “A container is a thing that holds other things” gives the genus (thing) and the differentia (holds other things).

Definitions can be real (claiming to state what a thing actually is) or nominal (stipulating how a term will be used). Most working definitions in a library are nominal — they fix usage for the purpose of building further claims. A real definition asserts something about the world; a nominal definition asserts something about language.

item.title: Document
item.description:
A document is a sign — physical or symbolic — preserved or recorded for the purpose of representing, reconstructing, or proving a phenomenon.

This is Suzanne Briet’s definition (Qu’est-ce que la documentation?, 1951). It is deceptively simple and radically expansive. A document need not be a text. It can be a photograph, a record, a map, an object, a file, a sound, a specimen. What makes something a document is not what it is made of but what it is preserved for.

item.title: Energy
item.description:
Energy is the capacity to act — what enables a thing to produce change.

The word comes from the Greek energeia, which Aristotle used to mean actuality or activity. It literally means “in-work” — en (in) plus ergon (work). When you have energy, you have the capacity to do work. When a thing has energy, it can produce change in itself or the world around it.

¶What It Is

In physics, energy is precisely defined: it is the capacity to do work, where work is force applied across distance. A moving object has kinetic energy — the energy of motion. An object held above the ground has potential energy — the energy of position. A hot object has thermal energy — the capacity to transfer heat. A chemical bond has chemical energy — the capacity to release itself. These are not different substances. They are different measures of a thing’s capacity to produce change.

item.title: Environment
item.description:
An environment is the whole of conditions surrounding a thing — what is around it, what it interacts with, what it is coupled to.

The simplest view: the environment is everything external to a thing that affects it or that it can act on. A plant’s environment is the soil, light, water, air, nearby organisms. A person’s environment is the city they live in, the people they know, the climate they experience. The boundary between the thing and its environment is clear: there is the thing itself, and there is everything around it.

item.title: Everything
item.description:
Everything is every thing taken together — the totality without exclusion or boundary.

The concept seems simple until you try to define it precisely. What is the whole? The universe? All that exists? But as soon as you formalize this, paradoxes appear. Mereology — the logic of parts and wholes — offers a framework: everything is the mereological sum of all parts. Transitivity, closure, structure. But mereology requires that you can actually identify all the parts, that parthood has determinate meaning. It works for bounded systems (a machine, an organism) but breaks when applied to the infinite.

item.title: Force
item.description:
A force is an influence that changes the state of a thing — its motion, shape, or condition. Newton formalized the physical case: a force is what causes a body with mass to accelerate, proportional to the mass and in the direction of the force applied. But the concept is older and broader than physics.

In mechanics, the four fundamental forces (gravitational, electromagnetic, strong nuclear, weak nuclear) account for all physical interactions. Gravity pulls massive bodies together. Electromagnetism holds atoms together and pushes magnets apart. The strong force binds protons and neutrons in a nucleus. The weak force governs radioactive decay. Every physical change traces back to one of these.

item.title: Information
item.description:
Information is a difference that makes a difference. Gregory Bateson’s formulation captures what Shannon’s mathematical theory formalized: information is what reduces uncertainty. A coin flip carries one bit of information because it resolves one binary question. A message that tells you nothing you didn’t already know carries zero information, no matter how many words it uses.

Shannon (1948) separated information from meaning. In his framework, information is a measure of surprise — the less probable a message, the more information it carries. This lets you quantify information without knowing what it’s about, which is why the same theory works for telegraph cables, DNA, and hard drives. The unit is the bit: the amount of information needed to distinguish between two equally likely outcomes.

item.title: Knowledge
item.description:
Knowledge is information that has been warranted through inquiry — not just accumulated data but understanding that has been tested, structured, and integrated.

Information is a difference that makes a difference; it reduces uncertainty. But information alone is not knowledge. A person can have access to all true information about a domain — every fact, every law, every principle — and yet lack knowledge of how to act, how to judge, how to recognize patterns in new contexts.

item.title: List
item.description:
A list is an ordered sequence of items treated as a single unit. The ordering may carry meaning (a ranked list, a procedure) or may be arbitrary (a grocery list, an inventory). What makes a list a list is that the items are gathered together and can be traversed from first to last.

Lists are among the most basic structures for organizing things. A container holds things spatially; a list holds things sequentially. Every table of contents is a list. Every set of instructions is a list. Every index, queue, ledger, and catalog is a list or is built from one.

item.title: Mass
item.description:
Mass is a measure of how much matter a thing contains. More precisely, it quantifies a body’s resistance to acceleration when a force is applied — what Newton called inertia. Push two objects with the same force: the one that accelerates less has more mass.

Mass also determines gravitational attraction. Every object with mass pulls on every other object with mass. The more mass, the stronger the pull. This is why mass and weight are easy to confuse — weight is the force of gravity acting on a mass, so it changes with location (you weigh less on the Moon), but mass stays the same everywhere.

item.title: Matter
item.description:
Matter is the substance of objects — anything that has mass and takes up space. If you can weigh it and it occupies volume, it is matter. Rocks, water, air, and plasma are all matter in different states.

Matter exists in four commonly encountered states: solid, liquid, gas, and plasma. What determines the state is how tightly the constituent particles (atoms and molecules) are bound by forces. In a solid, particles are locked in place. In a liquid, they slide past each other. In a gas, they fly freely. In plasma, electrons are stripped from atoms entirely. Temperature and pressure govern transitions between states.

item.title: Object
item.description:
An object is a thing that exists as physical matter. It has mass, takes up space, and can be observed. A rock, a cup, a chair, a person’s body, a planet — all objects.

Objects have two properties that non-physical things lack: they are massive (they have weight and resist being moved) and they are notable (they can be seen, touched, or otherwise sensed). A thought has neither property. A number has neither. An object has both.

item.title: Quality
item.description:
A quality is any aspect of a thing that can be observed, measured, or distinguished. Color, weight, temperature, shape, readiness, courage, solubility — all qualities. A quality makes a thing THIS thing and not another.

¶What Quality IS: Aristotle’s Four Species

Aristotle’s Categories Chapter 8 establishes the foundational account. Quality (poiotes) is one of ten categories of being. Aristotle distinguishes four species, each fundamentally different:

Habit or disposition (hexis): A stable, relatively permanent condition. Health in a body. Skill in a musician. Virtue in a person. These persist over time but can be lost or changed.

item.title: State
item.description:
A state is the composite of all conditions that define a thing at a given moment. It is what you would know about something if you had a complete, instantaneous snapshot of it right now.

A patient’s state includes their blood pressure, body temperature, recent symptoms, current location, and every other measurable fact about them at that moment. A chess game’s state is the position of every piece on the board plus whose turn it is. A trial’s state is its position in procedure: discovery phase, trial, appeal, concluded. The state is the whole picture of a thing as it is now.

item.title: Thing
item.description:
A thing is anything that can be distinguished from everything else. If you can point at it, name it, or talk about it, it is a thing. A rock is a thing. A color is a thing. A number, a feeling, a country, a mistake — all things. “Thing” is the most general word there is. Everything in this library is a thing.

A thing has qualities — properties that make it distinguishable. A red ball is distinguished from a blue ball by color. A heavy box is distinguished from a light box by weight. Without at least one quality, there is nothing to distinguish, and so nothing to call a thing.

item.title: Tool
item.description: A tool is an object used to extend or amplify a person’s capability. A hammer extends the force of an arm. A telescope extends the range of an eye. A computer extends the capacity to calculate, store, and retrieve information. What makes something a tool is not what it is made of but how it is used — the same rock is a paperweight, a weapon, or a tool depending on the intention behind its use.

item.title: Polyvalence
item.description:
Polyvalence is the condition of bearing multiple values, meanings, or interpretations simultaneously. A polyvalent sign, concept, or structure does not resolve into a single determinate meaning but sustains several at once — not as ambiguity (failure to determine) but as constitutive multiplicity. The sign that is polyvalent is not broken or vague. It is doing what signs do: meaning differently in different contexts while remaining recognizably the same sign.

In semiotics, polyvalence describes signs whose meaning cannot be reduced to a single referent or interpretation. A symbol may function differently in different contexts — the cross means different things in a church, a hospital, and a military cemetery — while remaining the same sign. Its polyvalence is not noise to be filtered out but a feature of how signification works. This is the fundamental distinction from ambiguity. An ambiguous sign has one intended meaning and others that are parasitic. A polyvalent sign has multiple meanings that are all genuinely its own.
